coredata.dc:title: Calibration of varying MoS2 amount with dual fabrication GCN/CQDs for supercapacitor’s electrode and strengthening of the energy parameters
coredata.dc:creator: Dhanda, Monika
Arora, Rajat
Yadav, Meena
Pahuja, Priti
Ahlawat, Simran
Nehra, S.P.
Lata, Suman
coredata.dc:description: A heptazine form of graphitic carbon nitride (h-g-CN) and Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), each was obtained through thermal polymerization procedure and furthered their nano-ranged duo-composite as g-CN/CQDs through hydrothermal method. The influence of different amounts of MoS2 insertions was studied on this duo after making its trio recipe (g-CN/CQDs/MoS2) as a super-capacitive working electrode for energy storing capability. After the ample characterization of the composites, these were tested for electrochemical applications.During the optimized study in a three-electrode system, it was found that CQDs and g-C3N4 in 1:2 (fixed) by varying MoS2 (30 %) shows the specific capacitance as 445.4F/g at 2 mV/s in 1 M H2SO4 with cyclic stability of 89.3 % even after 10,000 cycles. Theenergy density and power density were observed as 37.5 Wh/kg and 404.19 W/kg respectively, at1 A/g.In a two-electrode system with specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 as 122.5F/g at 0.1 A/g.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
MoS2
Super-capacitive energy
G-C3N4
Intercalated electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots activated with PSS (CQDs)
Electrochemical performance in two electrode system

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support granted to Monika Dhanda as a JRF by the University Grants Commission (UGC), Delhi, India (Ref. No. 201610158088 ). The authors are thanks to Rajat Arora and Simran Ahlawat for receiving a University Research Scholarship (Endst. No. DCRUST/Sch./2019/508 and Endst. No. DCRUST/Sch./2021/218-223 ) from Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of GCN/CQDs/MoS2.
(a) XRD of CQDs; (b) XRD of MoS2 (c) XRD of GCN, GCN/CQDs/10%MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20%MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30%MoS2, GCN/CQDs/40%MoS2.
(a) FT-IR of MoS2; (b) FT-IR of CQDs (c) FT-IR of GCN; (d) FT-IR of all samples.
Raman spectrum of GCN; (b) Raman spectrum of GCN/CQDs/30% MoS2.
BET analysis of GCN/CQDs/30% MoS2 (a) N2 absorption–desorption isotherms; (b) Linear fit plot for surface area; (c) Pore Volume versus pore diameter; (d) BJH adsorption differential pore area.
FESEM images of (a) GCN at x500; (b) CQDs at x500; (c) CQDs at x200; (d) GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at x10K; (e) GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at x30K; (f) Fluorescence evidence of CQDs.
TEM images of (a) GCN; (b) CQDs; (c) GCN/CQDs/30% MoS2.
(a) EDS; (b) Elemental mapping of GCN/CQDs/30% MoS2; Elemental mapping of (c) carbon; (d) nitrogen; (e) molybdenum; (f) sulpur.
(a) CV curves of all prepared samples; (b) CV curves of GCN/CQDs/30% MoS2 at different scan rate (mV/s).
(a) GCD of all prepared samples; (b) GCD of GCN/CQDs/30% MoS2 at the different current density.
(a) Ragone plot of GCN/CQDs/30% MoS2; (b) Specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30% MoS2 at a different scan rate (mV/s); (c) Cyclic stability of GCN/CQDs/30% MoS2 from 2 to 10,000 cycles.
(a) Nyquist plots of all samples; (b) Fitted and experimental data for GCN/CQDs/30%MoS2; (c) Electrical equivalent circuit.

(a) CV curves of all prepared samples in potential window 0.0 V to + 0.7 V; (b) CV curves of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at different scan rate (mV/s) in potential window 0.0 V to + 0.7 V; (c) CV curves of all prepared samples in potential window −0.7 V to 0.0 V; (b) CV curves of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at different scan rate (mV/s) in potential window −0.7 V to 0.0 V.

(a) GCD of all prepared samples in potential window 0.0 V to + 0.7 V; (b) GCD of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at the different current density in potential window 0.0 V to + 0.7 V; (c) Ragone plot of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 in two electrode system.
Optimization of the Mass ratio of various synthesized composites.
The specific capacitance and areal capacitance of different samples at 2 mV/s.
The specific capacitance and areal capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30% at different mV/s.
Fitted data of EIS for different samples.
The specific capacitance of different samples at 2 mV/s in 0.0 V to + 0.7 V.
The specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % at different mV/s in 0.0 V to + 0.7 V.
The specific capacitance of different samples at 2 mV/s in −0.7 V to 0.0 V.
The specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % at different mV/s in −0.7 V to 0.0 V.

head.ce:title: Calibration of varying MoS2 amount with dual fabrication GCN/CQDs for supercapacitor’s electrode and strengthening of the energy parameters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal 131039, Haryana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal 131039, Haryana, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Excellence for Energy and Environmental Studies, Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal 131039, Haryana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre of Excellence for Energy and Environmental Studies
Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Panipat Institute of Engineering and Technology, Samalkha 132102, Haryana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Panipat Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Panipat Institute of Engineering and Technology, Samalkha 132102, Haryana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A heptazine form of graphitic carbon nitride (h-g-CN) and Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), each was obtained through thermal polymerization procedure and furthered their nano-ranged duo-composite as g-CN/CQDs through hydrothermal method. The influence of different amounts of MoS2 insertions was studied on this duo after making its trio recipe (g-CN/CQDs/MoS2) as a super-capacitive working electrode for energy storing capability. After the ample characterization of the composites, these were tested for electrochemical applications.During the optimized study in a three-electrode system, it was found that CQDs and g-C3N4 in 1:2 (fixed) by varying MoS2 (30 %) shows the specific capacitance as 445.4F/g at 2 mV/s in 1 M H2SO4 with cyclic stability of 89.3 % even after 10,000 cycles. Theenergy density and power density were observed as 37.5 Wh/kg and 404.19 W/kg respectively, at1 A/g.In a two-electrode system with specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 as 122.5F/g at 0.1 A/g.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last decade, customer interest in commercially available portable devices such as flexible screens, television screens, and cellphones has surged [1]. The increasing growth of portable electronics has increased the demand for energy storage systems that are both versatile and efficient [2]. Among all such energy supplying systems, the Supercapacitors (SCs) [3] are a type of energy storage technology that has a lot of potential [4,5]. Because of their large storage capacity and efficient release of electrical energy, they are more efficient than traditional batteries [6]. SCs have a fast-charging process, a high-power density, great cycling stability, and a long service life due to their electrochemical properties [7,8]. Many factors influence the performance of SCs, including the electrochemical parameters of the electrode materials, the electrolyte [9] is chosen, and the electrodes' potential window [10,11]. The development of improved materials for SC electrodes with adequate structural features to promote successful electron transport and ionic diffusion has thus gotten a lot of attention [11].

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [12], which has exceptional optical and electrical properties, like most of the other 2D materials, has sparked a surge in scientific interest [13]. The interconnected s-triazine or tri-s-triazine units via weak van der Waals forces formed the 2D pi-conjugated network of g-C3N4 (GCN). For better electron donor–acceptor properties, the condensed pi-conjugated ring of nitrogen and carbon provides more active sites and wettability with electrolyte molecules [14]. However, due to the aggregation of the layered structure, it has a low electronic conductivity and a small surface area, which limits its use in electronic devices [15]. Couple with other transition metals to provide efficient charge transfer between electrode–electrolyte interfaces [16,17]. Many researchers are continuously working on this subject to remove all hurdles by altering morphology (increased surface area) [18], introducing heteroatoms, and other methods [19]. Ansari et al. reported hetero-structured electrode had a capacitance of 240.85F/g, which was higher than the bare material's (48.77F/g), and good capacitance retention [17]. Meng et.al synthesized oxygen-rich porous carbons via carbonization, acidic washing, and KOH activation processes derived from Alfalfa flowers [18]. The electrode made of carbons activated at 800 °C after acidic washing had the maximum specific capacitance of 350.1F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g and 297F/g at a current density of 50 A/g in a three-electrode system. Maciej tobis et al. fabricated carbon/MoS2 composites [19]. The synthesized samples were used as electrode materials in symmetric ECs, and their electrochemical performance was compared to that of pure carbon supports. At a current load of 0.2 A/g, NTs/MoS2 had the best electrochemical metrics in terms of conductivity and capacitance (150F/g), while BP2000/MoS2 had 110F/g at a current load of 0.2 A/g.

MoS2 is a well-known two-dimensional chalcogenide family member with three atomic layers [20,23] and weak van der Waals' interactions sandwiching the Mo atom between the two layers of S atom [20–22]. Because of its strong ionic conductivity [24], MoS2 has been used in capacitor technology. It stores charge on the individual atomic layers of MoS2 in a non-faradaic manner, while the changing oxidation state supplied by the Mo atom could also store charges in a faradaic manner [25]. For the first time, the authors of the present study report the fabrication of a GCN/CQDs/MoS2 by using a hydrothermal technique for synthesis where, firstly, the bi-composite as GCN/CQDs is prepared and then, insertions of variable doses of MoS2 in the optimized GCN/CQDs were processed to get the improved energy parameters. In this paper, we used 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte, the electrochemical performance of the synthesized material was investigated in a three-electrode as well as two-electrode configurations.

Urea (99.99 %), Polystyrene sulphonic acid (99.8 %) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Ascorbic acid (99.7 %) was obtained by Sisco Research. Molybdenum (IV) Sulfide (99.7 %), ethanol, sulfuric acid, and acetone were obtained from CDH. Polyvinylidene fluoride (99.99 %) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. In the whole process, double distilled water was used.

In order to get GCN, 5 g of urea was dissolved in 5 mL of distilled water in a glass beaker. Then put this beaker into a hot air oven at 80 °C. The obtained white powder was muffled at 550 °C for 4 h at a ramp rate of 5 °C/min [26]. Finally, the yellow powder as h-GCN, a hepta form was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots activated with PSS (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Polystyrene sulphonic acid (PSS) and ascorbic acid in a ratio of 1:2 was completely dissolved in distilled water and dried at 100 °C [27]. Thus, obtained semi-dry solid was placed in a vacuum oven to remove moisture. After that, the sample was kept in a muffle furnace at 180 °C for 2 h. Finally, the brownish bubbled sample as CQDs was obtained.

Synthesized GCN and CQDs were ultrasonicated in a ratio of 2:1 in 20 mL ethanol for 30 min. After ultrasonication, this mixture was shifted into a 200 mL distilled water beaker and was stirred for 10 min. Then x% MoS2 was poured into the solution, where x stands for 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 40 %. After that, the solution was stirred for 2 h. The obtained product was transferred into an autoclave and heated up to a temperature of 150 °C for 22 h as shown in Fig. 1 . The obtained product was washed with distilled water, ethanol, and acetone several times. The obtained samples are 2GCN/1CQDs/10 %MoS2, 2GCN/1CQDs/20 %MoS2, 2GCN/1CQDs/30 %MoS2, 2GCN/1CQDs/40 %MoS2 named as GCN/CQDs/10 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2. Table 1 shows the optimization of the Mass ratio of various synthesized composites.

MoS2 was doped in the binary composites of GCN and CQDs following hydrothermal method. With CQDs intercalated within the polymerization ring of GCN and the nitrogen of GCN having a tendency to form bonds with the metal of MoS2, (that can also be seen in the TEM image of composite in Fig. 7 (c)), as the final product of the synthesis has a compact yet flexible structure. There is also a good chance that all of the precursors will exhibit weak van der Waal forces of attraction as an additional potential for interaction.

The Rigaku Ultima IV (10°-80°) was used to conduct X-ray diffraction (XRD) on all of the above prepared samples. Within the range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1, the produced composites were subjected to Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy with Perkin Elmer spectrum 2. The synthesized composites were undergone Raman spectroscopy, assisted by Alpha300 Make –WITec within range of 400 cm−1 – 3000 cm−1 and composites also analyzed for BET analysis. By the use of a JEOL JSM-7610F Plus, all of the samples were subjected to field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) for surface morphology. With the assistance of a TECHNAI 200 kV TEM, TEM with EDS was exercised.

Fig. 2 (a) depicts the XRD of CQDs, a broad peak observed at 23.2 [27]. The broad peak shows that these particles of dots are very small in size. The diameter of this can be calculated with the support of Scherrer’s equation (D = 0.89λ/ βCosθ), here D is average crystalline size, λ is the wavelength of X-ray used, β is full width at half maximum and θ is the diffraction angle. The Scherrer’s equation the size of permits CQDs is 8.1 nm. Fig. 2 (b) demonstrates the XRD of MoS2 [28] . Fig. 2 (c) depicts the XRD of GCN, GCN/CQDs/10 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2. The main peak is observed at 27.5° in GCN associated with plain (002) depicts the graphitic stacking of C3N4 in its GCN [26]. In GCN/CQDs/10 % MoS2, the peak is observed at 14.2, 32.8, 39.6, 50.1, 58.5, 60.6 indicating the presence of MoS2 in GCN. Same pattern also observed for GCN/CQDs/20 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2. It has been found that as the amount of MoS2 is increased in GCN and CQDs, the intensity of GCN decreased. This shows good interactions between MoS2, GCN, and CQDs.

In Fig. 3 (a), demonstrates the FT-IR of MoS2. The broad absorption bands at 597 cm−1, 765 cm−1, 1153 cm−1, 1630 cm−1 are associated with MoS2 [29]. The peak at 463 cm−1 is elucidate the presence of the S–S bond. The broadband 3306 cm−1 belongs to O–H group. In Fig. 3 (b), represents the FT-IR of CQDs, the peaks are observed at 2932 cm−1, 3389 cm−1 due to C–H symmetric and asymmetric stretching and O–H stretching respectively [29]. In CQDs spectra, also clarify the fingerprint region of PPS (545 cm−1-1272 cm−1) [27], which confirms the presence of PSS in CQDs. The broadband at 1751 cm−1, is ascribed by the presence of C = O stretching vibrations of ascorbic acid. The band at 1673 cm−1 is associated with to C = O groups interaction with sulfonating groups (-SO3H) of PSS. The peak at 832 cm−1 is due to C–H out of the plane vibration for the para position but in CQDs shifting of maxima is at 836 cm−1. Fig. 3 (c) depicts the FT-IR of GCN. In the FT-IR spectrum of GCN, the absorbance at 811 cm−1 was associated with breathing modes of vibration of triazine ring in GCN [30,31]. Around 1200 cm−1 –1650 cm−1 band is assisted with an aromatic ring of C-N and C = N. The stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group of a surface of the water molecule and NH are associated by the broadband of 3000 cm−1 –3500 cm−1 [32]. Fig. 3 (d), shows the FT-IR of GCN, GCN/CQDs/10 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/40 %. In the spectrum of composites, it is found that as the amount of MoS2 increased, the intensity of the broadband decreased gradually. All the samples of composites show nearly the same FT-IR spectrum.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the Raman spectra of GCN show a broad and prominent peak at about 1684 cm−1. In Fig. 4 (b), In-plane E1 2g and out-of-plane A1g are the two MoS2 peaks that are visible. Peak locations at 382.8 cm−1 for E1 2g and at 409.2 cm−1 for A1g demonstrate the existence of MoS2 in the GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 composite. The sharpening of the Raman peak of MoS2 is attributable to the formations of GCN, CQDs and MoS2 heterojunctions, which promote crystallinity.

The surface area and pore size distribution of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 are evaluated using nitrogen gas adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77.35 K bath temperature. The type IV adsorption–desorption isotherm is depicted in Fig. 5 (a). The hysteresis twist in the relative pressure (P/Po) range of 0.48–0.95 shows that the given composite is mesoporous. A typical multi-point BET nitrogen adsorption plot for nanospheres with an estimated specific surface area of 48.295 m2/g is shown in Fig. 5 (b). A map of the pore volume versus Pore diameter is shown in Fig. 5 (c). An adsorption branch BJH plot for pore size distribution may be seen in Fig. 5 (d). GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 pores are 33.17 nm on average in size. In strong agreement with the TEM data, the BET investigation clearly confirms the particles' mesoporous nature, with a high surface area and good pore size.

Fig. 6 illustrates the FESEM images of GCN, CQDs, and GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at a voltage of 20 kV. Fig. 6 (a) exhibits tremella sheet-like morphology [26], which are detected for GCN. Fig. 6 (b-c) describes the FESEM of CQDs, showing the assemblies of particles [27]. Although the SEM images of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 (Fig. 6(d-e)) illustrates rough heterogenous sheet-like structure which shows its baggy and porosity-like morphology. According to Fig. 6 (f), CQDs appear to be geen fluorescent when exposed to UV light, which shows the fluorescence evidence for CQDs.

TEM pictures of GCN, CQDs and GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at various magnifications are shown in Fig. 7 . The tiny thin irregular two-dimensional form of GCN is seen in Fig. 7 (a)[26]. In Fig. 7 (b), represents the accumulation of carbon quantum dots. The TEM picture of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 was studied, small dots of CQDs (appearing in circles) on the GCN and MoS2 sheet as seen in Fig. 7 (c), confirms appropriate synthesis. The EDS and elementary mapping are depicted in Fig. 8 . The weight percentages of C, N, Mo, and S have been determined to be 56.96, 37.70, 1.09, and 2.50, respectively. GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 composite element mapping reveals a homogeneous distribution of C, N, Mo, and S in the sample. No further elements from the precursor were found, confirming the composite's purity and successful synthesis. Here, the presented data are TEM images of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 (as composite of GCN, CQDs and MoS2 at various magnifications. Interestingly, star-shape of the carbon quantum dots is deviated that may be due to the proper synthesis of composite and development of some kind of interaction among the precursors.

To investigate the electrochemical characterisation of GCN and their composites, FRA 32 M module-controlled NOVA programme on the electrochemical workstation PGSTAT 204 Autolab, Netherlands was used. Here, the counter electrode was a platinum electrode, the Ag/AgCl (saturated as 3 M KCl solution) was used as the reference electrode, although the working electrode was composed of 80 % with the prepared material + 10 % acetylene black + 10 % binder (PTFE) in DMF with sonication till the suitable homogeneous slurry was obtained [33]. The graphite electrode with dimensions of the active surface area of 1 cm × 1 cm was loaded with slurry with the help of a dropper. After that kept in the oven and dried at 70 °C for 18 h. After 18 h, the graphite electrode weighted with slurry was kept into the electrolyte solution (1 M H2SO4)[34,35]. The electrochemical behavior of electrode material [36] was studied with the help of CV, GCD, EIS with a three-electrode system [37]. The electrochemical performance of synthesized composites is calculated with the help of CV, GCD, EIS [18,38,39]. Specific capacitance can be measured through CV [40] by this equation [26,41] equation (1).
(1)
C v
=
∫ I d V
ν m Δ V
where CV is the specific capacitance in F/g, I is the current in A, ν is scan rate in mV/s, m is active mass loaded on graphite electrode area of 1 cm × 1 cm,
Δ
V is operating potential window.

CV is observed at several scan rates from 2 mV/s to 90 mV/s in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte with a potential window −0.5 V to + 0.5 V [42,43]. Despite the fact that the positive current zone (0 to + 0.5 V) for anodic oxidation and the negative current region (-0.5 V to 0) for cathodic reduction both varied. Fig. 9 (a) represents CV plots for GCN, GCN/CQDs, and their composites at a scan rate of 2 mV/s. It has been found that the GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 has a maximum integral area at 2 mV/s. The specific capacitance is 445.4F/g with an active mass of electrode (1.56 mg). The value of specific capacitance increases as the concentration of MoS2 rises to 30 %. This might be since MoS2 acts as spacers on the surface of GCN and CQDs. This not only prevents GCN and CQDs from aggregating, but also helps to maintain efficient ion transport routes. After 30 % of MoS2, the value of specific capacitance gradually drops due to the covering of most of the pores in GCN and CQDs, which slows the electron transfer rate between GCN and CQDs, resulting in a decrease in capacitance value. The value of specific capacitance is 32.1F/g, 62.4F/g, 53.3F/g, 81.2F/g, 113.8F/g, 445.4F/g, 172.9F/g for GCN, MoS2, GCN/CQDs, GCN/CQDs /10 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2 at a scan rate of 2 mV/s. Fig. 9 (b) depicts CV curves of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at various scan rates from 2 mV/s to 90 mV/s. It is observed that the area of CV curves increased with increasing the scanning rate. All the CV curves depict the rectangular area. The reduction and oxidation peak increases gradually, as the scan rate increases from 2 mV/s to 90 mV/s. The observed value of specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at scan rate of 2 mV/s, 5 mV/s, 10 mV/s, 20 mV/s, 30 mV/s, 40 mV/s, 50 mV/s, 60 mV/s, 70 mV/s, 80 mV/s and 90 mV/s are 445.4F/g, 327.2F/g, 173.3F/g, 131.4F/g, 121.6F/g, 116.7F/g, 113.1F/g, 111.8F/g, 104.9F/g, 102.2F/g and 99.3F/g respectively.

To elevate the areal capacitance with the help of cv plots by using equation [26] equation (2).
(2)
Ca
=
∫ I d V
ν A Δ V
where Ca = areal capacitance (F/cm2), I = current (A), ν = scan rate (mV/s),
Δ
V = operating potential window. The areal capacitance of GCN, GCN/CQDs, GCN/CQDs /10 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 and GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2 are 0.0012F/cm2, 0.0016F/cm2, 0.0027F/cm2, 0.0037F/cm2, 0.4310F/cm2 and 0.0061F/cm2 respectively. The value of areal capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 is much higher than the neat GCN.

The specific capacitance and areal capacitance of various samples at 2 mV/s are shown in Table 2 . The areal capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 of 2 mV/s is 0.43F/cm2. Table 3 depicts the specific capacitance and areal capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % at different scan rate (mV/s).

To analyze the electrochemical behavior of the synthesized composites, galvanostatic charge–discharge is observed in 1 M H2SO4 electrolytes at different current densities. By using GCD, the specific capacitance is calculated by equation [41] as shown below equation (3).
(3)
C d
=
I × Δ t
m × Δ V
where Cd is specific capacitance (F/g), I is current (A),
Δ t
is discharge time (s), m is active electrode material mass (g), V is the potential window (v).

Fig. 10 (a) depicts the GCD curves of GCN, GCN/CQDs, MoS2, and its composites at a current density of 1 A/g in potential window + 0.7 V to −0.2 V. By using the given formula, it is concluded, when we consider other parameters are constant, specific capacitance directly proportional to discharge time. GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 has the maximum discharge time (293.8 sec) with the highest specific capacitance of 334F/g at a current density of 1 A/g. The specific capacitance of GCN, GCN/CQDs, MoS2, GCN/CQDs/10 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2 are found 10.1F/g, 11.9F/g, 12.1F/g, 52.8F/g, 92.8F/g, and 262.5F/g respectively. Fig. 10 (b) illustrates the GCD curves of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at altered current density. The value of specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 is 122.4 F/g, 90.6 F/g, 73.3 F/g, and 61.3 F/g at a current density of 1.5 A/g, 2.0 A/g, 2.5 A/g, and 3.0 A/g respectively. Fig. 11 (a) shows the Ragone plot of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2. Fig. 11 (b) depicts the specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at a different scan rate (mV/s).

Fig. 11 (c) demonstrates the cyclic stability of GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 composite. Cycling stability is observed by continuously repeating CV plots in an operating window of −0.5 V to + 0.5 V in a 1 M H2SO4 solution. It is estimated that cycle retention 95.9 % after 2000 cycles, 92.5 % after 4000 cycles, 90.2 % after 8000 cycles, 89.3 % after 10,000 cycles. It is noticed that the present trio of three in-gradients of the working electrode displayed a significantly enhanced specific capacity as compared to the earlier reported similar study [18] with good retention even after 10,000 loops.

To elucidate the relationship between energy density and power density is calculated by given equation [44] equation (4) and (5).
(4)
E =
1
2 × 3.6
× C
Δ V
2
(5)
P = 3600 ×
E t

It is found that, power density directly proportional to energy density. The energy density for GCN, GCN/CQDs, GCN/CQDs/10 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 and GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2 are 1.14 Wh/kg, 1.43 Wh/kg, 5.94 Wh/kg, 10.44 Wh/kg, 37.5 Wh/kg and 29.5 Wh/kg respectively. The power density for GCN/CQDs/10 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 %MoS2 GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 and GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2 are 282.15 W/kg, 449.5 W/kg, 404.1 W/kg and 450.9 W/kg. Both, the energy density as well as power density were examined at the current density 1A/g. Fig. 11 (a) depicts the Ragone plot for GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 that provides related behavior of energy density and power density.

The frequency and resistance characteristics of GCN/CQDs, GCN/CQDs/10 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 and GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2 were studied using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In a 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution [45], this measurement was evaluated at a frequency range of 105 to 0.1 Hz [46]. Table 4 shows the different parameters of EIS fitted data for several samples. The Nyquist plots for GCN/CQDs/10 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2, and GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2, are shown in Fig. 12 (a), where Z' and Z“ represent real and imaginary impedance, respectively [47]. Fitted and experimental results for GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 are shown in Fig. 12 (b), with the lack of a semicircle indicating the least circulation of charge storage. Fitting experimental impedance data to an equivalent electrical circuit is required for a complete understanding of the Nyquist plot. The corresponding electrical circuit is shown in Fig. 12 (c), is utilized to fit data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance in two electrode system
ce:section.ce:para:
Equation (6) displays the formula for specific capacitance derived from CV in a two- electrode setup.
(6)
C v
= 2
∫ I d V
ν m Δ V
where CV is the specific capacitance in F/g, I is the current in A, V is the working potential window, and m is the active mass applied to the electrode in mg.

In a solution of 1 M H2SO4 [47,48] the analysis of the cyclic voltammograms is carried out at various scan rates ranging from 2 mV/s to 100 mV/s across the voltage range of −0.7 V to 0.0 V and 0.0 V to + 0.7 V. In −0.7 V to 0.0 V, at a scan rate of 2 mV/s and an active electrode mass of 1.51 mg, it has been reported that the sample GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 displays a specific capacitance of 56.2F/g. The specific capacitance values for GCN, MoS2, GCN/CQDs, GCN/CQDs/10 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 %MoS2, and GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2 are 5.4F/g, 8.4F/g, 8.2F/g, 17.14F/g, 25.4 F/g and 47.2F/g at at a scan rate of 2 mV/s respectively. The CV curves of GCN, MoS2, GCN/CQDs, GCN/CQDs/10 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 %MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 and GCN/CQDs/40 % in a two-electrode system are shown in Fig. 13 (a), along with CV curves of GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 at various scan rate mV/s. The specific capacitance of various composites at 2 mV/s in a two-electrode system(0.0 V to + 0.7 V) is shown in Table 5 . The specific capacitance data of GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 are 54.1 F/g, 45.4 F/g, 31.8 F/g and 19.8 F/g at scan rate of 5 mV/s, 10 mV/s, 20 mV/s and 30 mV/s respectively as in Table 6 .

In −0.7 V to 0.0 V, at a scan rate of 2 mV/s and an active electrode mass of 1.51 mg, it has been reported that the sample GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 displays a specific capacitance of 20.3F/g. The specific capacitances for MoS2, GCN/CQDs, GCN/CQDs/10 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 % MoS2, and GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2 are 6.5F/g, 1.3F/g, 10.1F/g, 11.2F/g and 13.7F/g, respectively. Fig. 13 (a) depicts the CV curves of all prepared samples; (b) CV curves of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at different scan rate (mV/s). Table 7 displays the specific capacitance values of several composites at 2 mV/s in a two-electrode setup (-0.7 V to 0.0 V). Table 8 represents the specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 at different scan rate (mV/s) in a two-electrode setup.

To study the electrochemical performance of the produced samples, galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) is observed in a two-electrode setup. The specific capacitance may be computed using GCD, according to equation (7).
(7)
Cd
= 4
I × Δ t
m × Δ V
I = current in ampere, t = discharge time in seconds, m = active mass applied to electrode in mg, and V = working potential window. Cd = specific capacitance in F/g along GCD curves.

Fig. 14 (a) shows the GCD curves for different samples in 0.0 V + 0.7 V potential window at 0.1 A/g current density. The value of specific capacitance at a current density of 0.1 A/g for GCN, MoS2, GCN/CQDs, GCN/CQDs/10 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/20 % MoS2, GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 and GCN/CQDs/40 % MoS2 is 3.2F/g, 5.42F/g, 18.1 F/g, 27.4 F/g, 61.7 F/g, 122.5 F/g and 92.1 F/g respectively. Fig. 14 (b) represents the GCD of GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 at the various current density. The specific capacitance of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 is 122.5 F/g, 110.2 F/g, 81.9 F/g and 25.4 F/g at 0.1 A/g, 0.2 A/g, 0.3 A/g and 0.4 A/g respectively.

The power density and energy density are determined at various current densities using the previously discussed equations (4) and (5). At a current density of 0.1 A/g of GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2, the values of energy density and power density are 8.3 Wh/kg and 139.2 W/kg, respectively. At a current density of 0.2 A/g, 0.3 A/g and 0.4 A/g, the value of energy density are 7.5 Wh/kg, 5.6 Wh/kg and 1.7 Wh/kg, respectively and the power density are 236.2 W/kg, 419.7 W/kg and 559.9 W/kg. The Ragone plot of GCN/CQDs/30 %MoS2 in a two-electrode setup is shown in Fig. 14 (c).

The current work investigates the most effective approach for forming well conducting GCN/CQDs/x% MoS2 composites using hydrothermal method. These composites are designed through, first combining GCN and CQDs as bi-composite and thereafter, insertions of variable contents of MoS2 in the optimized GCN/CQDs and tested as supercapacitor electrodes, and going through the various characterizing methods such as XRD, FTIR, FESEM, TEM, BET, RAMAN spectra, followed by electrochemical procedures. Among the many notable characteristics of these composites, superior charge storage capacity and simple electron transit have promoted the composites to an efficient material for electrodes. GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 demonstrated to be significantly better super capacitive materials than the other composites with different dosage of MoS2 investigated, and thus, the findings may aid in the exploration of new electrodes for better practical applications. At a scan rate of 2 mV/s in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte, GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 is found to give an optimum value with specific capacitance and areal capacitance of 445.4F/g and 431.1 mF/cm2 respectively with cyclic stability of 89.3 % after 10,000 cycles. This might be owing to the composite's unusual structure, which increases the contact area between electrode material and electrolyte and provides more active sites for electrochemical processes. At a current density of 1 A/g, the energy density and power density values of GCN/CQDs/30 % MoS2 were 37.5 Wh/kg and 404.19 W/kg, respectively. Even, the 2-electrode system study also provides better outputs in addition to that of the 3-electrodes parameters for electrical energy storing capacity.

Monika Dhanda: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Validation. Rajat Arora: Formal analysis, Data curation, Resources. Meena Yadav: Conceptualization, Resources. Priti Pahuja: Methodology, Data curation. Simran Ahlawat: Methodology, Data curation. S.P. Nehra: Supervision, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Suman Lata: Conceptualization, Supervision, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The authors are thanks to Rajat Arora and Simran Ahlawat for receiving a University Research Scholarship (Endst. No. DCRUST/Sch./2019/508 and Endst. No. DCRUST/Sch./2021/218-223) from Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polypyrrole-covered MnO2 as electrode material for supercapacitor
